CLARITY.justification: The paper is generally well-organized (Intro → Related Work → Environment → Experiments → Conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early in a clear bullet list (end of §1). However, presentation quality is degraded by missing/placeholder figure/table references (e.g., "Figure ()", "Table (3)"), duplicated and garbled text near the end, and some inconsistent/awkward phrasing (e.g., "it’s games", repeated sentences). Notation is mostly usable (the Q-update is given) but the equation formatting and some variable explanations are loose; overall readable but several editing and formatting issues make parts hard to follow.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper's primary novelty is an RL environment (RLE) that extends beyond ALE by integrating LibRetro to support many consoles (SNES, Genesis, etc.) and thus thousands of additional games, introducing substantially harder visual/action spaces (Sec. 3.2–3.5). It also exposes multi-agent capabilities and a practical training strategy (alternating opponents) that empirically improves robustness (Sec. 4.3), which is a notable methodological contribution beyond mere engineering. The authors clearly contrast RLE with prior platforms (ALE, OpenAI Gym/Universe, Malmo, DeepMind Lab) and highlight differences (local execution, richer consoles) throughout Related Work. Overall this is a useful and well-differentiated extension of benchmarking infrastructure—important and non-trivial engineering with some novel training-perspective, but not a fundamentally new learning algorithm.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims (introducing RLE, SNES/Genesis are more challenging, reward shaping and multi-opponent training can help) and provides experimental evidence (game-case descriptions, F-Zero/Mario reward-shaping examples, multi-agent results with quantitative scores). Mathematical content is minimal and the single Q-update shown is standard/correct. However the experimental methodology has weaknesses: important details and full results/tables/figures are missing in the provided text, evaluation keeps epsilon-greedy during testing (contrary to common practice), training budgets appear smaller/underspecified relative to prior work, and there are no statistical tests or multiple random seeds reported. These issues create gaps in rigor and limit confidence in some claims (e.g. ‘‘only one of five’’ games surpassing humans, and robustness improvements), so the contribution is plausible but not fully substantiated.
